text: The surgeon encounters adhesions of the omentum to the gallbladder, which is tensely distended and difficult to grasp. Adherent pericolic fat and additional omentum are visible, and the surgeon carefully dissects these tissues to better identify the gallbladder.
The surgeon exposes the gallbladder wall by carefully peeling down the omentum, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon draws up the duodenum to improve visibility, revealing the bottom of the gallbladder.

The surgeon begins working in the area around the gallbladder, attempting to clear the peritoneum. The gallbladder appears chronically thickened and inflamed, making it challenging to identify the normal dissection planes.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the gallbladder, carefully opening the lateral peritoneum to improve retraction and exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon inadvertently creates a small hole in the gallbladder, revealing it is filled with pus, indicating infection or inflammation.
The surgeon continues to dissect in the area to locate the bottom of the gallbladder and its junction with the cystic duct, encountering dense and unforgiving tissue that complicates the process.

The surgeon identifies the borders of Calot's triangle and attempts to clear the tissue on the backside where the cystic duct is anticipated. The dissection is challenging due to difficult angles and the gallbladder's movement.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery and attempts to free it from surrounding tissue, aiming to facilitate a less bloody dissection and create more space for maneuvering.
The surgeon examines the peritoneum on the inferior surface of the duct, carefully assessing its appearance before confidently excising the tissue.
The surgeon positions the instrument directly on top of the duct, navigating the surgical field to identify and expose the structure.
The surgeon performs a cholangiogram through the hole in the gallbladder, confirming the anatomy before proceeding to clip and transect the cystic duct.
The surgeon identifies a posterior structure as the posterior cystic artery, visible behind the gallbladder during the dissection.
The surgeon proceeds to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed, carefully separating the gallbladder tissue from the liver surface.
